explanation: IB2-15 states that when end-user title-updates are disabled ("Allow User updates to Titles upon creation checkbox is disabled ... end user cannot update title names ... they see no overlay") the flow should behave as if the feature is not enabled and child records are created immediately, with an info snackbar confirming child record creation. By contrast, IB2-20 says that when the child database configuration is 'broken' (title field changed to non-text) or when end-user config does not currently display, "no child records are created, no snackbar error messaging is needed (current behaviour)". These describe mutually exclusive outcomes for the same observable condition (no overlay / no end-user config): IB2-15 => create child records + snackbar; IB2-20 => do not create child records and no snackbar. Implementing both as-written is impossible without changing one of them.
suggested_fix: Clarify and align acceptance criteria: explicitly enumerate the distinct scenarios that can cause the end-user config overlay to be absent (a) intentionally disabled via parent form config, (b) broken/invalid child form schema, etc., and define the expected behavior for each. For example: - If overlay is intentionally disabled in parent config, create child records immediately and show the 'Linked Records are being created' snackbar (as IB2-15). - If the overlay is absent due to invalid child schema, fail-safe by NOT creating child records and show a non-blocking admin-facing audit/error log (or surface a specific error snackbar) as described in IB2-20. Update both stories' acceptance criteria and add automated tests to verify each scenario (including snackbar ordering and presence/absence), or merge the two stories into a single story with conditional branches to remove ambiguity.

explanation: IB2-164 requires the Drag & Drop Page limit to be available "in the Restrictions tab in MCP or Custom Page" and specifies it should "be shown somewhere on Custom Page." IB2-389 explicitly states Page limits must be set "via MCP settings, not in Custom Page." These are mutually exclusive requirements about where the page-limit configuration lives and whether the Custom Page can host the setting, so both cannot be implemented as-written.
suggested_fix: Clarify the authoritative UX and configuration location: either (A) make MCP the single source-of-truth for page limits and update IB2-164 to state MCP sets the limit and the Custom Page only displays the effective limit (read-only), or (B) allow page-level overrides in Custom Page and update IB2-389 to permit that. Update acceptance criteria to state source-of-truth, precedence/override rules, and exact UI behavior (where the control appears and whether Custom Page can edit it).

explanation: IB2-78 requires that when a Server deletion fails the confirmation dialog remains open and a snackbar appears with the error message. IB2-172 explicitly requires that when deletion fails the confirmation dialog must close automatically and no snackbar error message should be displayed. These are mutually exclusive UI behaviors for the same failure case.
suggested_fix: Product/UX should clarify the canonical failure UX for Server delete actions across MCP. Options: (A) Close the confirmation dialog on failure and surface an inline error (no snackbar) as IB2-172 prescribes, or (B) Keep the dialog open and show a snackbar error as IB2-78 prescribes. Update both stories' acceptance criteria (Gherkin) to state the chosen behavior consistently and remove ambiguity in IB2-78's delete clause (the 'dialog remains open(?)' note). Ensure the chosen pattern is applied consistently to create/update/delete failure flows and documented in the UX pattern guide.

explanation: IB2-211 states: "Module appears if it applies to User’s usage report, whilst in Custom Page usage report the Module will not be displayed" (i.e., the Module/column should NOT be shown in the Custom Page usage report). IB2-248 describes reporting for Simple/Custom Page Create action and lists both "User Usage Report" and "Custom Page Usage Report" as targets for the Create action report, implying the page/module reporting fields (such as the Module column) will be available in the Custom Page usage report. These two statements contradict each other about whether the Module field/column should be displayed in the Custom Page usage report.
suggested_fix: Clarify and align reporting scope in the product spec: produce a reporting matrix that lists each tracked action (Create, Resource Download/Preview, etc.) and, for each action, indicate whether it appears in User Usage Report and/or Custom Page Usage Report and whether the Module column/field is shown. Update IB2-211 and IB2-248 acceptance criteria to reference that matrix and make explicit which report(s) should show the Module and which should not.

explanation: IB2-80: 'If either no Master templates exist, then the ‘No Master Templates available’ message displays and user can select to Create a Blank Template instead.' vs IB2-337: 'The “Start with a blank template” option must only be displayed when at least one Master Template exists... If no Master Templates are available, users must not be allowed to create a blank template; instead, display a message instructing users to create a Master Template before continuing.' These two stories prescribe opposite UX/authorization for the 'Create a Blank Template' option when no Master Templates exist.
suggested_fix: Resolve which behavior is desired and update both stories: either (A) allow 'Create Blank Template' when no Master Templates exist (update IB2-337 acceptance criteria and designs), or (B) disallow 'Create Blank Template' and require creating a Master Template first (update IB2-80). If needed, add conditional rules (e.g., allow only Admins to create blank templates) and capture that in acceptance criteria, UI designs, and role-based examples. Ask PM/UX to choose and then align both stories and associated tests.

explanation: IB2-463 requests a mechanism to allow 'complete removal of existing facial data and related flags' (e.g., a checkbox to wipe existing data) during MCP Site cloning. IB2-476 requires that 'Existing Unknown face flags must be preserved during clone handling so the “Unknown People” filter can still return matching Image Resources' and that Unknown People filters remain available after cloning. If the clone operation includes an unconditional wipe or if the wipe is not clearly opt-in with safeguards, the preservation guarantee in IB2-476 would be violated. The two stories make incompatible claims about the outcome of cloning on facial data unless the clone behavior (default and opt-in/opt-out) is explicitly defined.
suggested_fix: Clarify clone semantics in acceptance criteria: specify the default behavior (e.g., preserve facial data/flags by default) and define the wipe option as an explicit, opt-in action with required confirmation and audit logging. Update IB2-463 to state that the wipe is optional and must not occur unless explicitly selected, and update IB2-476 to note any allowed exceptions (e.g., explicit admin wipe) so both stories can be implemented without contradiction.

explanation: IB2-115 asks for clarity on “the new widget settings from Nav (combining previously defined Block and Widget's settings) for each widgets.” IB2-305 implements "Be able to see and configure the new widget settings from Nav (combining previously defined Block and Widget's settings) ... Button: Button Text, Button Link." The acceptance intent and scope (combined Block+Widget settings for Button) are the same, so completing IB2-305 would largely satisfy IB2-115 and render it redundant.
suggested_fix: Consolidate IB2-115 into IB2-305: merge IB2-115's clarification tasks into IB2-305's acceptance criteria (add explicit documentation / help copy and any clarification acceptance steps). Close IB2-115 or convert it to a small documentation/discovery subtask under IB2-305 to avoid duplicate implementation work.

explanation: IB2-189's acceptance points (“display a visual outline ... full Widget boundary ... default 8px spacing”, “external border visible ... after image dimensions are adjusted”, “position within its Block area to remain proportional after resizing ... preserve that proportional placement if the Block width later changes”) mirror IB2-117 almost verbatim. Implementing IB2-117 would produce the same UI behavior specified in IB2-189.
suggested_fix: Merge the two tickets into one canonical Image Widget reposition/resize story (keep the most complete acceptance criteria). Close one as duplicate and ensure references/assignees are consolidated.

explanation: IB2-190 asks that after resizing/repositioning the Image by dragging, users “see the same percentage horizontally of Image with the Block in mobile, tablet, and desktop modes,” which overlaps with IB2-117's requirement that the image retains the same percentage of the Block and that the percentage persists if Block width changes. The responsive-mode framing is the main difference but the technical behavior requested appears the same.
suggested_fix: Either merge IB2-190 into IB2-117 as a responsive-mode acceptance criterion, or update IB2-117 to explicitly call out responsive-mode expectations and reference IB2-190 so work is not duplicated.

explanation: IB2-189 point 3 requires preserving proportional image placement after resizing and when the Block width changes. IB2-190 requires that the image maintain the same horizontal percentage within the Block across mobile, tablet, and desktop after resizing/repositioning. These describe the same technical behavior (percentage-based positioning persistence across responsive modes) and would likely result in duplicated effort if implemented separately.
suggested_fix: Merge or explicitly de-duplicate: make IB2-190 an acceptance criterion or test case within IB2-189 (or vice-versa). Add concrete test matrix for responsive modes (mobile/tablet/desktop) and expected percentage-based positions so a single implementation and test suite satisfy both tickets.

explanation: IB2-21 specifies the core Bulk Apply selection behaviour: resources de-selected must not receive updates when 'Apply to Selected' is used. IB2-240 is a Bulk Apply story for Grouped work that calls out 'Actual apply logic' and matching behaviour to the Grouped Review page. Both stories appear to require implementing the same selection/apply semantics for bulk updates; completing one likely renders the other's core implementation redundant unless the scope is explicitly separated.
suggested_fix: Clarify and consolidate: either merge IB2-21 into the broader IB2-240 Bulk Apply epic (and ensure IB2-21 acceptance criteria are included), or update IB2-240 to explicitly reference and exclude the deselect-specific behaviour so IB2-21 remains a distinct, narrow story. Ensure acceptance criteria explicitly state selection semantics in the chosen parent story.

explanation: IB2-448 also describes adding Bulk Apply functionality for updates introduced through Grouped work and explicitly requires the same behaviour as the existing Grouped Review page, including 'same apply processing logic'. This overlaps with IB2-21's requirement that de-selected items must not receive updates when 'Apply to Selected' is used, indicating duplicate implementation effort for selection/apply logic.
suggested_fix: Consolidate or decompose: make IB2-448 the parent story that includes the deselect behaviour from IB2-21, or convert IB2-21 into a subtask/acceptance criterion of IB2-448/IB2-240. Update story descriptions so each has a non-overlapping scope (e.g., UI-only vs. backend apply logic).

explanation: IB2-215 and IB2-385 both describe the same deliverable: enabling drag-and-drop reordering of pages within the Navigation panel (within the same parent/root), moving all child pages with a parent when the parent is moved, and collapsing sub-pages under the parent after reordering. IB2-215: 'Be able to re-order Pages via drag-and-drop from Nav Panel during editing', 'Sub-pages will be moved all together with Page', 'After drag-and-drop... Sub-pages will be collapsed'. IB2-385: 'Allow users to change the order of Pages by using drag-and-drop interactions within the Navigation panel while editing', 'When a Page contains child pages, move all associated sub-pages together with the parent Page', 'ensure all Sub-pages are displayed in a collapsed state beneath their parent'. Implementing one makes the other redundant.
suggested_fix: Consolidate these two stories into a single ticket (merge acceptance criteria and implementation notes). Close or mark one as duplicate and ensure the merged story includes explicit FE/BE integration points, edge cases (e.g., root vs. sub-page ordering), and the 'no cross-parent drag-and-drop' scope note if that restriction should remain.

explanation: IB2-415 describes the same UI feature as IB2-225: clicking the settings button for a simple page in the Nav, showing the sub-page popup, and inputting & saving the sub-page title (change 'Done' to 'Save'). The core deliverable (create sub-page via Nav) is identical — completing IB2-225 would make IB2-415 redundant.
suggested_fix: Close or mark IB2-415 as a duplicate of IB2-225 and consolidate acceptance criteria into IB2-225 (include placeholder, validation, Save label, and ordering requirements currently present in IB2-225).

explanation: Both stories request the same UI feature: an extend/collapse button to open the Left Side Panel as the Page Navigation and the ability to see all saved Simple Pages in that Left Side Panel (including permission filtering). IB2-233 and IB2-429 contain near-identical descriptions (e.g., 'see the new extend / collapse button icon ... open the Left Side Panel as Page Nav' and 'see all the saved Simple pages from the Left Side Panel against current user’s permission'), so completing one would make the other redundant.
suggested_fix: Consolidate the two stories into a single canonical story (keep IB2-233 as the primary or merge IB2-429 into it). If one was intended to be a FE/BE split, convert the duplicate into explicit FE or BE subtasks and ensure unique acceptance criteria (e.g., backend APIs, permissions checks, or UI visuals) are retained in the appropriate task.

explanation: IB2-240 and IB2-448 request the same feature: Bulk Apply for updates introduced through Grouped work. Both specify identical UI/behaviour requirements (apply button item counts, button hover tooltip text for enabled/disabled states, snackbar notifications) and the same apply-processing logic and validation/Complete Request/Stage determination. Implementing one would fully satisfy the other's deliverables.
suggested_fix: Consolidate the two stories into a single canonical story (keep one key, close the other as duplicate). Merge any differing acceptance criteria or implementation notes into the remaining story (explicitly include the tooltip/hover behaviour, snackbar messages, apply logic, validation checks, and Complete Request/Stage rules). Assign a single owner and ensure dependent stories reference the consolidated story.

explanation: IB2-448 requires the Bulk Apply experience to 'follow the same behavior as the existing Grouped Review page' and to 'support the same apply processing logic used in the current implementation.' IB2-455 specifies the checkbox selection behavior for bulk-approvable requests (only relevant checkboxes remain selectable when a Pending request is selected). The selection/checkbox rule is behavior that appears to be part of the Grouped Review page UX and apply processing, so implementing IB2-448 would likely include the exact functionality described in IB2-455.
suggested_fix: Clarify and consolidate scope: either make IB2-455 a sub-task/acceptance criterion of IB2-448 (if it's the same behavior), or explicitly differentiate the work (e.g., IB2-455 covers only checkbox selection controls across views while IB2-448 covers apply-processing logic). Update acceptance criteria to show the single source of truth for selection behavior.

explanation: Acceptance text is ambiguous: it describes a flicker-like behaviour but doesn't define the expected final state, timing, or conditions. The team cannot reliably estimate effort without knowing whether this is a fix (remove flicker) or an intentional animation.
suggested_fix: Rewrite ACs to specify the exact expected behaviour (e.g., 'Page Category label must remain visible; no disappearance on page open' OR 'label should animate with X duration and Y easing'). Include failure criteria and repro steps for current behaviour.

explanation: Because the request is an open question rather than a scoped change, the team cannot estimate effort or implementation approach.
suggested_fix: Define the expected deliverable(s) (UI tooltip text, help doc, or a mapping matrix) and include examples of properties to be explained; then the team can estimate.

explanation: The last acceptance criterion is truncated/unclear: 'the percentage will obtain if the 'Block' width changes by' — missing the rule/formula and edge cases. Without a precise definition of how percentage positions are calculated, effort and complexity are unknown.
suggested_fix: Clarify the exact positioning/percentage behaviour with formulas or examples (e.g., 'image X% from left remains X% after block resize; if block shrinks by Ypx then image offset recalculates as...'). Add repro scenarios and expected behaviour for min/max sizes.

explanation: The description is terse and ambiguous ('show a logo, but not the log-in or nav menu... like the CPSL links') and lacks precise acceptance criteria (which screens, responsive behaviour, accessibility, and failure states). The team cannot estimate effort from this text alone.
suggested_fix: Expand the story to specify all screens involved (login, success page), exact UI elements to show/hide, designs or mocks, accessibility requirements, and browser support. Add ACs for visual verification and tests.

explanation: The story is ambiguous about what 'Drag & Drop Page limit' actually means (limit of pages, items, file count, characters?), which entity the limit applies to, units/defaults, and the exact MCP UI behavior. Without those specifics the team cannot reliably estimate implementation effort or acceptance.
suggested_fix: Clarify the beneficiary and measurable definition: state exactly what is being limited (e.g., max pages per Nav, max image uploads per Drag & Drop Page, or max widgets), the units and default value, expected enforcement behavior and error/validation messages, where in MCP it should be configurable (exact tab/field), and acceptance criteria (Gherkin) including relevant NFRs (e.g., limits to support).

explanation: The acceptance is incomplete and vague ('for each widgets:' with no list). It is unclear which Block and Text Widget properties must be combined, how they surface in the Nav, and which settings are new vs. legacy, so the team cannot size or plan the work.
suggested_fix: Provide a complete, explicit list of widget/block properties to expose and how they map to the Nav UI (include before/after examples or design mocks). Add clear acceptance criteria (Gherkin) for each property, expected persistence, permission rules, and any NFRs (e.g., performance, supported browsers).

explanation: The story contains contradictory and unclear statements about where the Module should appear and how 'Custom Page' naming is determined. The visibility rules and exact reporting fields (what constitutes a 'Resource Download (Preview)') are not defined, preventing reliable estimation.
suggested_fix: Clarify the exact reporting requirements: define the event schema for 'Resource Download (Preview)', list which reports (User Usage, Custom Page Usage) should include the module and under which conditions, resolve the contradictory visibility statements, provide the dynamic naming rule from Admin, and add acceptance criteria including export/search expectations.

explanation: Core 'apply' logic is not specified ('Actual apply logic' is missing) and the dependence of Complete Request/Stage on request staging is ambiguous. Because this describes business logic for bulk operations, the missing rules block design and estimation of correct, safe implementation.
suggested_fix: Provide detailed rules and examples for the bulk 'Apply' flow: specify the exact state transitions, how counts display, tooltip behavior, snackbar expectations, and conditions under which requests/stages are marked complete. Include Gherkin scenarios covering single vs bulk, staged vs non-staged requests, error handling, and any performance NFRs for bulk sizes.

explanation: The description is incomplete and fragmentary (missing concrete acceptance criteria and scenarios). It references multiple possible reviewer configurations across folders but does not define exact expected behaviours, edge cases, or outputs for grouped email updates.
suggested_fix: Clarify and expand the acceptance criteria with concrete scenarios (e.g., example folder combinations, expected recipient lists, email templates, handling of conflicting reviewer sets). Add Gherkin scenarios for each cross-folder case and expected notification outcomes so the team can estimate effort.

explanation: The story states 'set Page limits on MCP control panel' but doesn't define what limits (per-page, per-site, max pages, page-size, asset limits), how they are enforced, or measurable targets. Without these specifics the team cannot reliably estimate effort or impacts.
suggested_fix: Specify the exact limits to be supported (types and numeric targets), the enforcement rules (when and where enforced, error messages), admin UI expectations, and any reporting/audit requirements. Add acceptance criteria specifying enforcement behavior and any NFRs (e.g., performance, multi-tenant defaults).

explanation: The description is fragmented and ambiguous (e.g. 'Change from to exclude V2 Approval List...' and incomplete sentence about Requesters). It does not clearly state the exact rule, the UI/UX surface affected, or expected acceptance criteria, so the team cannot estimate implementation effort with confidence.
suggested_fix: Clarify the desired behavior with explicit acceptance criteria: state the exact rule (when Requesters can/cannot re-assign), which lists/components are in/out (V2 Approval List behavior), examples of allowed/blocked actions, expected UI messages, and any permission matrix changes. Include any NFRs (audit/logging) if applicable.

explanation: The desired behavior is unclear/contradictory: title suggests hiding Decline in some cases but the description says to still display Decline when selected items are mixed. The acceptance condition (when to show/hide Decline and how Bulk Apply should treat inapplicable items) is not unambiguous, blocking reliable estimation.
suggested_fix: Define the exact business rule with examples: specify when 'Decline' must be hidden vs shown in Bulk Review (e.g., hide if ALL selected items have Hide Decline enabled; if mixed, disable option with tooltip vs show partial application). Add acceptance criteria and expected UI states/labels and any validation behavior for bulk operations.

explanation: The story claims 'all editable fields' should show in 'My Response' but does not list which fields or the rules that determine visibility (it references 'below fields' but none are included). Without a concrete list and visibility rules, the scope and effort are unclear.
suggested_fix: Provide a definitive list of the fields to surface in 'My Response', include the visibility conditions for each (based on request params), example scenarios, and expected field types (e.g., rich text vs plain). Add acceptance criteria (Gherkin/scenarios) showing when each field should appear/hide and any validation rules.

explanation: Functional intent is clear (list Smart Pages via API) but the non-functional expectation 'response time should be mostly instant' is vague and untestable. Without measurable performance targets, pagination/size limits, auth/filters, or expected payload shape, the team cannot reliably estimate backend, paging, or caching work required.
suggested_fix: Define explicit acceptance criteria: expected API contract (fields returned), pagination limits (page size, offsets), max response payload size, a measurable latency SLA (e.g., 95th percentile < 300ms for pages of 20 items), required filters/sorting, auth scopes, and supported browsers/clients. Add example requests/responses and performance test requirements.

explanation: The description is vague and uses non-committal language ('should probably', 'may show', 'may autoplay'). Key behaviors are unspecified (when preview appears, playback controls visible or hidden, autoplay policy, fallback when MP4 conversion is pending/fails, performance limits, browser support), so the team cannot reliably estimate effort or QA work.
suggested_fix: Clarify acceptance criteria with concrete, testable requirements: e.g., when a GIF/Lottie has a converted MP4 available, show a 16:9 preview on the Share screen with play/pause controls hidden or visible (specify), autoplay boolean (on/off) and whether it should loop, define behavior while conversion is pending (placeholder/disabled preview), define NFRs (max file size, supported browsers, load/perf targets), and add error/fallback behavior. Provide mockups and Gherkin scenarios.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-245's core acceptance requires the new Bulk Actions checkbox column to appear only when an MCP development setting is enabled and at least one template has the new template-level setting. IB2-75 is the story adding the MCP setting for Approvals List Bulk Actions. The column's runtime visibility and acceptance tests therefore depend on IB2-75 being implemented.
suggested_fix: Plan IB2-75 to be implemented (and released to a testable environment) before or alongside IB2-245. Alternatively, include a controllable feature-flag stub in IB2-245 so QA can validate the column behaviour without the final MCP setting, but keep the dependency explicit in sprint planning.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-326 explicitly says the Ask button must only be displayed after the AI Assist visibility rules and the 'Limit to current view' search-scope logic are available. Its acceptance criteria require the Main-Admin visibility restriction (IB2-318), the search-scope logic (IB2-299), and the Ask-button behaviour/UI described in IB2-323. Without those prerequisite stories implemented, QA cannot validate the required visibility, scope usage, or enable/disable behaviour — IB2-326 cannot be accepted independently.
suggested_fix: Treat IB2-326 as dependent work: implement and release IB2-318 (AI Assist visibility), IB2-299 (Limit-to-current-view logic) and IB2-323 (Ask button UI state) first, or split IB2-326 into (A) gating/visibility checks that depend on IB2-318/299 and (B) post-gate integration work. Alternatively add a test-only toggle to simulate the visibility/scope behavior for validation, but keep the story dependent in planning.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-3 creates the MCP toggle 'Resources - GIF/Lottie Preview Support' and states that any GIF/Lottie preview tickets in this project are enabled/disabled by that setting. IB2-9 implements the GIF/Lottie converted-MP4 preview on the Share screen. Because IB2-3 gates availability of GIF/Lottie preview features, IB2-9's acceptance (showing preview when feature enabled; absence when disabled) cannot be fully validated without the MCP setting being created or an equivalent test toggle.
suggested_fix: Implement the MCP toggle from IB2-3 first, or provide a test-only flag so IB2-9 can be validated independently. Alternatively, split IB2-9 into: (A) core preview implementation that can be tested behind a local/dev flag, and (B) final gating/integration that verifies behavior with the MCP setting.

explanation: The story explicitly depends on other stories: it requires the AI Assist visibility rules and the “Limit to current view” search-scope logic to be implemented first. As written it cannot be completed or properly tested in isolation.
suggested_fix: Declare and link the dependent stories/requirements in the story (e.g., IB2-318 and IB2-299). Either convert this into a follow-up story that runs after those are done or include fallback behavior so it can be implemented/tested independently (e.g., a mocked visibility/scope flag for local testing).

explanation: The acceptance notes explicitly state this success-state behavior must wait until IB-382 (simplified header state for public users) is completed. That external dependency prevents independent delivery/testing of this story.
suggested_fix: Document the dependency on IB-382 in the story, or split: (A) implement randomized presentation logic and preview mode (independent), and (B) integrate it with the simplified public header once IB-382 is done. Include test hooks to verify the randomized presentation independently.

explanation: [NOT_INDEPENDENT] IB2-366's acceptance explicitly requires reusing the public-user page header layout that is produced by IB2-382 (it even states it 'should wait until IB-382 completed'). The randomized success confirmation depends on the simplified header state introduced by IB2-382, so IB2-366 cannot be validated or delivered independently.
suggested_fix: Implement IB2-382 (hide/adjust header for public users) before IB2-366, or scope IB2-366 to deliver the randomized confirmation only for non-public pages and add a follow-up integration story to apply the behavior once IB2-382 is done.

explanation: This story bundles many distinct verticals: MCP toggle, provider switch logic, migration of Imagga configs and history, billing/quota changes, analytics/reporting, notifications, API/permissions changes, and documentation/rollout. These are multiple separable deliverables that cross backend, billing, analytics and docs and cannot reasonably be completed and validated as a single sprint story.
suggested_fix: Split into focused stories (examples): 1) Add MCP toggle 'Enable Image Tagging (Azure)' and provider-switch logic; 2) Add Azure image processing limits (images) and rename video limit units; 3) Migrate Imagga image-tagging configurations and usage history to Azure (data migration task); 4) Update billing/quota tracking for Azure image processing; 5) Add analytics/reporting for image-tagging provider activity; 6) Add notifications for limits/quota exhaustion/provider failures; 7) Update APIs, permissions and documentation. Each child story should include clear acceptance criteria and NFRs (performance, migration validation, rollback plan).

explanation: The story bundles multiple large responsibilities: schema/field changes (include Q/A/Rating), renaming, filtering dev servers, historical data migration, multi-environment aggregation, backward compatibility, and automated sync across reporting systems. This is multi-workstream and too large for a single sprint story.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller stories/technical tasks: (a) schema and report rename + unit tests, (b) filter out dev servers and update generation logic, (c) implement historical data migration plan (with migration scripts and verification), and (d) multi-environment aggregation and export sync (separate integration story). Provide acceptance criteria and migration rollback plan for each.

explanation: The story bundles many large, distinct concerns (drag-and-drop UI, migration of child references/permissions, URL/breadcrumb/SEO updates, undo/redo, version history, audit logging, notifications, workflow updates, role-based permissions, cross-API sync, mobile clients). This is an epic-sized scope, not a single sprint story.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller vertical slices, for example: (1) UI: drag-and-drop and navigation update (frontend-only), (2) Data migration: move child references and update URLs/SEO, (3) Permissions & workflows: migrate permissions and update workflows, (4) Audit/logging and notifications, (5) Undo/redo and version history, (6) Cross-client sync and API contracts. For each slice include clear acceptance criteria and NFRs.

explanation: The story bundles multiple large capabilities (view/configure sub-pages, permission propagation, hierarchical inheritance, multi-level propagation, approval workflows, audit history, and cross-platform synchronization). This is an epic-sized scope that cannot reliably be completed in a single sprint.
suggested_fix: Split into smaller, independently valuable stories (for example: (A) UI to list/configure sub-pages under Admin->Group, (B) permission propagation algorithm & basic single-level inheritance, (C) multi-level propagation with testing and audit logging, (D) approval-workflow integration for permission changes, (E) synchronization to external platforms). For each split, add clear acceptance criteria and NFRs.

explanation: The story mixes a configuration toggle for grouped requests with many cross-cutting capabilities (global template inheritance, multilingual generation, tenant-level customization, real-time delivery tracking, approval workflows, and external sync). This conflates basic enablement with multiple advanced features and integrations—too large for one story.
suggested_fix: Separate the core change (add 'Enable Grouped Requests' toggle and surface basic notification options) from the advanced capabilities. Create follow-up stories for each advanced requirement (multilingual templates, delivery tracking, external provider sync, tenant overrides). For the core story, include concrete acceptance criteria and the specific notification options expected.

explanation: The story describes the Page Category briefly disappearing/reappearing on view but does not explain why that behavior is desired or what user problem is solved. It reads like a bug-description without a business outcome.
suggested_fix: Clarify whether this is a UI bug to be fixed (and what correct behaviour should be) or an intentional animation/UX feature. State the user benefit and acceptance criteria (expected visual behaviour, timing thresholds, browsers supported).

explanation: The item is phrased as a question / uncertainty rather than a user story or requirement. It lacks a clear persona, goal, or measurable outcome, so it cannot be treated as a deliverable user story.
suggested_fix: Convert into one or more concrete stories: e.g., 'As a [persona], I want documentation or UI labels that explain combined Block/Button widget properties so I can configure widgets correctly.' Add acceptance criteria listing which settings need explanations and where (tooltips, docs, inline help).

explanation: The story requires integrated fields to briefly disappear and reappear but does not explain the user or business outcome this behavior delivers. The requested transient hide/show appears like a UI quirk with unclear benefit and may harm UX if implemented without rationale.
suggested_fix: Explain the business/user outcome for the hide/show behavior (e.g., visual refresh to show updated external data, security masking, or performance workaround), define timing/animation details and measurable acceptance criteria (how long, under what triggers, accessibility considerations), and include any NFRs (performance, browser support).

explanation: The story requests hiding the 'Stickers' menu option but does not state who benefits or what tangible business/user outcome is expected (e.g., reduced clutter, removed feature for permission reasons). It reads like a UI tweak without a stated value.
suggested_fix: Add the business outcome and acceptance criteria: who should no longer see the item, why it's being removed, any permission or rollout constraints, and verification steps (screens/users to check).
